rulings.Case Title: CREDITORS TRADE ASSOCIATION, INC. DBA VS. ROBERT MARCO CORSI et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Pursuant To Ccp 583.250(A) For Failure To Serve Summons Within Three Years Of Commencement Of The Action; Motion To Quash Service And Vacate Default Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, February 5, 2015, Line 1, DEFENDANT ROBERT CORSI's Motion To Dismiss Pursuant To CCP 583.250(A) For Failure To Serve Summons Within Three Years Of Commencement Of The Action; Motion To Quash Service And Vacate Default Judgment. Denied. The motion to set aside the default judgment is untimely. Defendant argues that the default judgment should be set aside because service was not properly effectuated. A motion for relief from a judgment that is valid on its face but void for improper service is governed by analogy to CCP sec. 473.5 and relief must be sought no later than two years after entry of the default judgment. (Rogers v. Silverman (1989) 216 Cal.App.3d 1114, 1124; see also Weil and Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civ. Proc. Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2013) par. 5:491.) Default judgment was entered in December 2008. Defendant's motion to set aside default judgment was filed in December 2014, well beyond the two year period. The motion to dismiss pursuant to CCP sec. 538.250(a) is denied. "Service is complete when all of the required acts are done." (Ginns v. Shumate (1977) 65 Cal.App.3d 802, 805; see also Weil and Brown, Cal. Practice Guide: Civ. Proc. Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2013) par. 11:64, "For dismissal purposes defendant is 'served' when plaintiff has performed all acts required for substitute service under CCP sec. 415.20. As long as these acts are completed within the 3-year period, the action is not subject to mandatory dismissal.") On December 15, 2008, Plaintiff filed a proof of service that shows that all of the acts required for substitute service were performed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: LEVELLA JOHNSON et al VS. MARYALICE ARRE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Sanctions For Failure To Comply With Court Order Compelling Discovery; Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 5, 2015, Line 1 - DEFENDANT MARIA GOMEZ, ROCIO GOMEZ, JOEL GOMEZ's Motion For Terminating Sanctions For Failure To Comply With Court Order Compelling Discovery; Request For Monetary Sanctions. (Part I of II) Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: "[A] trial court may order a terminating sanction for discovery abuse 'after considering the totality of the circumstances: [the] conduct of the party to determine if the actions were willful; the detriment to the propounding party; and the number of formal and informal attempts to obtain the discovery.' [Citation.] Generally, '[a] decision to order terminating sanctions should not be made lightly." Los Defensores, Inc. v. Gomez (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 377, 390. The Court finds that Plaintiff Levella Johnson has failed to comply with this Court's orders of September 22, 2014 requiring her to 1) serve written verified answers and responses (without objection other than privilege) to moving parties' Special Interrogatories, Limited Form Interrogatories and Requests for Production of Documents; and 2) to appear for deposition prior to October 20. Pursuant to CCP 2030.300(e) and 2031.300(c), the Court GRANTS the motion in part as follows: Within 10 days of the date of service of this order on Plaintiff, Plaintiff Levella Johnson shall serve written verified answers and responses (without objections) to moving parties' Special and Limited Form Interrogatories and Requests for Production of Documents; and 2) shall appear for deposition prior to February 27, 2015. The Court advises Plaintiff Levella Johnson that if she fails to comply with this Order (i.e. does not provide Defendants' counsel with written verified answers and responses to the Special and Limited Form Interrogatories and Requests for Production of Documents and does not appear for deposition), her lawsuit against Defendants Rocio, Joel and Maria Gomez may be dismissed due to her repeated failure to participate in discovery. Defendants' motion for a terminating sanction is DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE and may be renewed if Plaintiff fails to comply with this order. Defendants' request for $559.48 in monetary sanctions is DENIED for failure to state in the motion the basis for the calculation of the requested amount of sanctions. See CCP 2023.040 (requiring declaration setting forth facts supporting the amount of any monetary sanction sought). (To be continued to Part II) =(D302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARYANNE MORCH VS. SURGICAL CARE AFFILIATES, LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses, Document Production And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, February 5, 2015, Line 2 - PLAINTIFF MARYANNE MORCH's Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses, Document Production And Request For Sanctions. (Part I of II) Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: RPD 46-The objection on privacy grounds to providing insurance information is improper. While the Legislature has stated that insurance information may not be admissible, it has also specifically announced that certain specific types of insurance information may be properly discovered. Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.210. To the extent that it has not already been produced, Defendants shall provide Plaintiff with a copy of any insurance policy under which Defendants believe Defendants were or might be insured for Plaintiff's claims. See Catholic Mut. Relief Soc. v. Superior Court (2007) 42 Cal 4th 358 (copies of defendant's insurance policies produced to plaintiff under case management order pursuant to section 2017.210). Int 216.1-Interrogatory responses were verified on April 11, starting a 45 day clock for a motion to compel further responses to such interrogatories. See Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.300 (c). Defendants on May 22, 2014 agreed in writing to "supplement [their] response [to Interrogatory 216.1] at this early stage of discovery and ¿to continue [to] supplement th[e] response as they discover facts that are responsive to this interrogatory." Stehr Dec., Exh. E. However, there is no evidence of any written agreement to extend the deadline to bring a motion to compel in connection with this interrogatory response or any supplemental response. Accordingly, the motion is denied as the deadline to compel further responses to this interrogatory has passed. The denial of the motion is without prejudice to Plaintiff seeking before the trial judge exclusion or some other sanction for any evidence that would have been disclosed in a supplemental response to this interrogatory pursuant to the May 22, 2014 agreement if no such supplemental response has been timely provided by Defendants. (To be continued to Part II) =(D302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GREGORY CHANDLER VS. STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The State Bar Of Californias Special Motion To Strike Complaint Under Californias Anti-Slapp Statute [C.C.P. 425.16]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, February 5, 2015, Line 9, DEFENDANT STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA's Special Motion To Strike Complaint Under Californias Anti-Slapp Statute [C.C.P. 425.16]. Granted. The Court must engage in a two-step process when ruling on an anti-SLAPP motion under CCP sec. 425.16. "First, the court decides whether the defendant has made a threshold showing that the cause of action is one arising from protected activity¿If the court finds such a showing has been made¿it then determines whether the plaintiff has demonstrated a probability of prevailing on the claim." (Equilon Enterprises v. Consumer Cause Inc. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 53, 67.) In determining whether the allegations arise from a protected activity, the court's "focus is on the principal thrust or gravamen of the causes of action, i.e., the allegedly wrongful and injury producing conduct that provides the foundation for the claims." (Castleman v. Sagaser (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 481, 490-91.) Here, each of Plaintiff's causes of action arises out of the State Bar disciplinary proceedings brought against him or the State Bar conducting an arbitration and enforcing an arbitration award, as required by Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 6200 et seq. Thus, the first prong of the anti-SLAPP statute is met. Plaintiff has not demonstrated a probability of prevailing. A plaintiff is required to "show both that the claim is legally sufficient and there is admissible evidence that, if credited, would be sufficient to sustain a favorable judgment." (McGarry v. University of San Diego 154 Cal.App.4th 97, 108.) The only evidence submitted by Plaintiff is his declaration. The declaration restates the allegations in the complaint and cites to jury instructions. The declaration does not state facts, as required by CCP sec. 425.16(b)(2). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
